section.title: The Dreams in Prison
section.sectionRationale: In prison Joseph meets Pharaoh's cupbearer and baker, who each dream a troubling dream. Insisting that interpretations belong to God, Joseph reads the dreams truly: restoration for one, execution for the other. His plea to be remembered goes unanswered, and the chapter closes on a forgotten man in a dungeon.
section.thesis: God, not human wisdom, reveals and rules the future; He gives Joseph true interpretations even in prison, yet ordains that his release must wait on God's timing rather than on a grateful man's memory.
section.frameNote: Four movements: two royal prisoners and their dreams; the cupbearer's dream interpreted; the baker's dream interpreted; and the dreams fulfilled on Pharaoh's birthday, with Joseph forgotten.

verses.text: Some time later, the king’s cupbearer and baker offended their master, the king of Egypt.
Pharaoh was angry with his two officers, the chief cupbearer and the chief baker,
and imprisoned them in the house of the captain of the guard, the same prison where Joseph was confined.
The captain of the guard assigned them to Joseph, and he became their personal attendant. After they had been in custody for some time,
both of these men, the Egyptian king’s cupbearer and baker, who were being held in the prison, had a {{chalom|dream}} on the same night, and each dream had its own meaning.
When Joseph came to them in the morning, he saw that they were distraught.
So he asked the officials of Pharaoh who were in custody with him in his master’s house, “Why are your faces so downcast today?”
“We both had dreams,” they replied, “but there is no one to interpret them.” Then Joseph said to them, “Don’t {{pithron|interpretations}} belong to God? Tell me your dreams.”
So the chief cupbearer told Joseph his dream: “In my dream there was a vine before me,
and on the vine were three branches. As it budded, its blossoms opened and its clusters ripened into grapes.
Pharaoh’s cup was in my hand, and I took the grapes, squeezed them into his cup, and placed the cup in his hand.”
Joseph replied, “This is the interpretation: The three branches are three days.
Within three days Pharaoh will lift up your head and restore your position. You will put Pharaoh’s cup in his hand, just as you did when you were his cupbearer.
But when it goes well for you, please {{zakar|remember}} me and show me kindness by mentioning me to Pharaoh, that he might bring me out of this prison.
For I was kidnapped from the land of the Hebrews, and even here I have done nothing for which they should have put me in this dungeon.”

units.label: The baker's dream
units.note: a favorable start, a fatal end

verses.text: When the chief baker saw that the interpretation was favorable, he said to Joseph, “I too had a dream: There were three baskets of white bread on my head.
In the top basket were all sorts of baked goods for Pharaoh, but the birds were eating them out of the basket on my head.”
Joseph replied, “This is the interpretation: The three baskets are three days.
Within three days Pharaoh will lift off your head and hang you on a tree. Then the birds will eat the flesh of your body.”
On the third day, which was Pharaoh’s birthday, he held a feast for all his officials, and in their presence he {{nasarosh|lifted up the heads}} of the chief cupbearer and the chief baker.
Pharaoh restored the chief cupbearer to his position, so that he once again placed the cup in Pharaoh’s hand.
But Pharaoh hanged the chief baker, just as Joseph had described to them in his interpretation.
The chief cupbearer, however, did not remember Joseph; he forgot all about him.

chalom.gloss: A dream. Dreams structure the whole Joseph story, and here two God-given dreams in a single night set the stage for Joseph's eventual rise. In Genesis, dreams are a means by which God discloses what He is about to do, so their true interpretation belongs to Him alone.

pithron.gloss: Interpretation, the meaning of a dream. Joseph deflects glory from himself with the question, do not interpretations belong to God, setting Israel's God above the celebrated dream-diviners of Egypt. The word ties this chapter to the next, where the same God-given gift lifts Joseph before Pharaoh.

zakar.gloss: To remember, to call to mind and act upon. Joseph asks the cupbearer to remember him, but the man forgets, verse 23. The contrast is pointed: human memory fails, yet God will remember Joseph in His own time, as He remembered Noah and the covenant, driving the story toward deliverance.

nasarosh.gloss: To lift up the head. Joseph uses this idiom two ways: for the cupbearer it means restoration and honor, for the baker a grim reversal, to lift off the head in execution. On Pharaoh's birthday both are lifted up, one to life and one to death, a sober wordplay on the two destinies decided by the king.

basic.context: Still in prison, Joseph is put in charge of two of Pharaoh's officials who have fallen out of favor, the cupbearer and the baker. Each has a dream on the same night. Joseph interprets them: the cupbearer will be restored, the baker executed, both within three days. It happens just as he said, but the cupbearer forgets him.
basic.hermeneutics: The key question is who holds the future and its meaning. Joseph answers plainly: interpretations belong to God. The chapter is not about Joseph's cleverness but about the God who reveals what He is about to do and governs the timing of everything, including Joseph's release.
basic.originalLanguages: Dreams are God's chosen way of disclosing His plans here, and their interpretation belongs to Him. Joseph asks the cupbearer to remember him, but the man forgets, while God will remember Joseph in His time. The phrase lift up the head is used two ways, for the cupbearer's honor and the baker's execution.
basic.typology: The two prisoners, one saved and one condemned on the same day, foreshadow the two criminals crucified beside Jesus, and the plea remember me anticipates the thief's cry to Christ. The three days and the lifting up quietly echo the pattern of death and resurrection.
basic.christ: Joseph, the innocent prisoner who announces salvation and judgment, points to Christ, between whom two condemned men met opposite ends. One asked Jesus to remember him and was received into paradise. The three days and the lifting up whisper of the greater deliverance won at the cross and empty tomb.
basic.god: God is the true interpreter and ruler of the future. He sends the dreams, gives the meaning, and brings both to pass. He also governs the delay, ordaining that Joseph wait longer, teaching that God's people are sustained by His remembrance rather than by the gratitude of others.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel echoes that God reveals mysteries and interprets dreams. Luke records two criminals crucified with Jesus, one crying remember me. Psalm 105 shows God's word testing Joseph until the appointed time of his release.
basic.oneStory: This chapter moves Joseph one step closer to the throne, even as it ends in apparent disappointment. God is arranging the pieces, revealing His power over the future and preparing the moment when He, not a forgetful cupbearer, will lift Joseph up to save many lives.

context.literary: The chapter is a hinge in the Joseph narrative, linking his imprisonment to his exaltation. It develops the dream motif from Genesis 37 and sets up Pharaoh's dreams in Genesis 41. The paired dreams, with their contrasting outcomes, and the closing note of forgetfulness heighten the tension before the turning point.
context.historical: The cupbearer and baker were high royal officials whose access to Pharaoh's food and drink made them trusted, and dangerous if disloyal. Egyptian culture prized dream interpretation, employing professional dream-readers, which makes Joseph's insistence that interpretation belongs to God pointed. Pharaoh's birthday feast fits known royal customs of amnesty and judgment.
context.canonical: The theme that God alone reveals and interprets mysteries recurs with Daniel in Babylon, another Hebrew exile who honors God before a foreign court. The scene of two condemned men and the plea to be remembered finds a striking echo in the crucifixion accounts of the Gospels.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: Moses shows that Israel's God, not Egypt's diviners, reveals and controls the future, that He equips Joseph even in prison, and that He, not human gratitude, will bring about deliverance in His appointed time.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes God's dealings and Joseph's faithfulness; it does not prescribe dream interpretation as a practice for believers. Its abiding lessons are God's sovereignty over the future, the call to give Him the glory, and trust in His timing amid disappointment.

debates.question: Was Joseph's request that the cupbearer remember him a lapse of faith or a proper use of means?

views.label: A proper, humble use of means
views.case: Asking for help is not unbelief; Joseph honestly states his innocence and seeks a lawful path out of unjust imprisonment, trusting God while acting wisely. The cupbearer's forgetting, not Joseph's asking, is the fault the text underlines.

views.label: A momentary leaning on man
views.case: Some see Joseph's plea, and the extra two years that follow, as a gentle lesson that his rescue would come from God's hand and timing, not from a courtier's favor, so that all glory would go to God.

debates.bearing: Both agree God's providence, not the cupbearer, secures Joseph's freedom. Believers may rightly use ordinary means while resting their ultimate hope in God, who remembers His own at the perfect time.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter turns on chalom, dreams, God's chosen way of revealing what He is about to do, and on pithron, interpretation, which Joseph insists belongs to God rather than to Egypt's diviners. Joseph asks the cupbearer to zakar, remember, him, but the man forgets, a pointed contrast with the God who remembers His people in due time. And the idiom nasa rosh, to lift up the head, is turned two ways on Pharaoh's birthday, honor for the cupbearer and execution for the baker, a sober wordplay on the two destinies the king decrees.

typology.type: Two condemned men, one restored and one executed
typology.fulfillment: The two criminals crucified beside Jesus, who meet opposite eternal ends.

typology.type: The plea remember me from a prisoner facing his fate
typology.fulfillment: The dying thief's cry, Jesus, remember me, answered with a place in paradise.

typology.type: Three days and the lifting up of the head
typology.fulfillment: The pattern of death and resurrection fulfilled in the crucified and risen Christ.

study.christ: Joseph, the innocent sufferer in prison, proclaims a word of salvation and a word of judgment, and on the third day both come true, one man lifted up to life and one to death. The scene foreshadows Calvary, where the innocent Christ hangs between two condemned men who meet opposite ends, and one pleads, Jesus, remember me, receiving the promise of paradise that the forgetful cupbearer could never give. The motifs of three days and being lifted up point beyond Joseph to the cross and resurrection, where God's deliverance is finally accomplished and remembered forever.
study.god: God stands behind every detail. He sends the two dreams in one night, grants Joseph their true meaning, and brings both to pass exactly on the third day. He is sovereign not only over the future but over its timing, allowing Joseph to be forgotten by man so that his exaltation, when it comes, will clearly be God's doing. The God who seems slow is not absent; He remembers His servant and is preparing salvation on a scale Joseph cannot yet imagine.

crossReferences.note: The cupbearer at last remembers Joseph, in God's timing, opening the way to Pharaoh.
There is a God in heaven who reveals mysteries, echoing Joseph's confession that interpretations belong to God.
Two criminals crucified with Jesus; one asks to be remembered and is welcomed into paradise.
The word of the LORD tested Joseph until what he foretold came to pass.

study.oneStory: This prison scene edges Joseph nearer the throne from which he will save the world's peoples from famine. God reveals His mastery over the future and its meaning, and even the sting of being forgotten serves His plan, ensuring that Joseph's rise will be seen as God's work. The chapter's quiet echoes of two condemned men, a plea to be remembered, and the third day point ahead to the cross, where God accomplishes and never forgets the salvation of His people.

quiz.q: How does Joseph respond when the prisoners say no one can interpret their dreams?

options.text: He asks, do not interpretations belong to God, then offers to hear them.
options.note: He points away from himself to God as the true interpreter, verse 8.

options.text: He boasts of his own skill in reading dreams.
options.note: He credits God, not himself; the point is God's revelation.

options.text: He refuses to get involved with their dreams.
options.note: He willingly hears and interprets, trusting God to give the meaning.

options.text: He tells them dreams are meaningless.
options.note: He treats the dreams as God's genuine disclosure of the future.

quiz.q: What happened to the two officials, just as Joseph foretold?

options.text: The cupbearer was restored and the baker executed, within three days.
options.note: Both came to pass on Pharaoh's birthday, verses 20 to 22.

options.text: Both were restored to their positions.
options.note: Only the cupbearer was restored; the baker was executed.

options.text: Both were executed for offending Pharaoh.
options.note: Only the baker died; the cupbearer was reinstated.

options.text: Both were released and forgotten.
options.note: One was restored and one killed, exactly as interpreted.

quiz.q: What is the significance of the cupbearer forgetting Joseph?

options.text: It shows human memory fails, but God will remember Joseph in His own time.
options.note: The delay ensures Joseph's rise is clearly God's doing, not a courtier's favor.

options.text: It proves Joseph's interpretation was wrong.
options.note: The interpretation came true; the forgetting is a separate, human failing.

options.text: It means God had abandoned Joseph.
options.note: God had not abandoned him; He was arranging a greater deliverance.

options.text: It ends Joseph's story permanently.
options.note: The story continues; the cupbearer remembers him in the next chapter.

quiz.q: How does this prison scene foreshadow the cross of Christ?

options.text: Two condemned men meet opposite ends, and one pleads to be remembered.
options.note: It echoes the two criminals beside Jesus and the thief's cry, Luke 23.

options.text: Joseph offers a sacrifice to atone for the prisoners.
options.note: There is no sacrifice here; the parallel is the two destinies and the plea to be remembered.

options.text: Pharaoh releases all prisoners in an act of mercy.
options.note: Pharaoh restores one and executes another; there is no general release.

options.text: The chapter has no connection to the cross.
options.note: Its echoes of two condemned men, remember me, and the third day point to Calvary.

quiz.q: What does the chapter teach about God and the future?

options.text: God alone reveals and controls the future, including its timing.
options.note: He sends the dreams, gives their meaning, and fulfills them on the third day.

options.text: The future is shaped mainly by skilled human interpreters.
options.note: Joseph insists interpretation belongs to God, not to human skill.

options.text: The future is random and cannot be known.
options.note: God knows and discloses it precisely in this chapter.

options.text: Egyptian diviners hold the true knowledge of dreams.
options.note: The chapter sets Israel's God above Egypt's diviners.

questions: Joseph gave God the credit before he said a word about the dreams. Where do you need to point away from your own ability and toward God in the gifts others praise in you?
Joseph served two hurting men faithfully even while imprisoned himself. How can your own hard place become a place of noticing and helping others?
The cupbearer forgot Joseph, and God's timing stretched on. How do you keep trusting God when your hopes are pinned on people who let you down?
One prisoner was restored and one condemned on the same day. How does this sober picture of two destinies shape the way you think about your standing before God?
The dying thief's plea, remember me, was answered by Christ. What does it mean to you that Jesus remembers and receives those the world forgets?

translationNotes.note: Lift up your head is a Hebrew idiom for showing favor and restoring to honor; the same phrase is turned to a grim sense in verse 19, lift off your head, heightening the contrast between the two fates.
Hang you on a tree may also be rendered impale you on a pole; the point is public execution followed by the shame of exposure to the birds.
In their presence he lifted up the heads of both officials draws together the two meanings of the idiom, one lifted to honor and one to execution, on the same day.
